locked_facts: Transverse wave: particle displacement perpendicular to propagation
Wave speed v = √(T/μ) where T = tension, μ = linear mass density
Frequency and wavelength related: v = fλ
Particles undergo SHM in transverse direction
truth_statements: In a transverse wave, particles vibrate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation
The speed of a transverse wave on a string depends on tension and linear mass density
The relation v = fλ holds for all types of waves
Each particle of the medium executes simple harmonic motion
Transverse waves can be produced on strings and surfaces but not in gases

aha_moment.what_to_show: One particle tracked (orange) — moves ONLY up/down while wave moves horizontally. Two arrows: particle motion ↕ (vertical, orange), wave propagation → (horizontal, blue). Perpendicular directions made explicit.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Tracked particle moves only vertically. Wave moves only horizontally. The two directions are perpendicular — this is what 'transverse' means. Particle does NOT travel horizontally with the wave.

simulation_states.label: Wave on a string — observe the pattern
simulation_states.what_student_sees: A transverse wave travelling rightward on a string. Smooth sine shape. Wavelength λ marked.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: This is a transverse wave on a taut string. The wave pattern moves horizontally.

simulation_states.label: Focus on one particle
simulation_states.what_student_sees: One particle on the string highlighted in orange. Watch it carefully as the wave passes through.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Track this one particle. How does it move? Does it travel with the wave, or does it do something else?

simulation_states.label: Key insight — perpendicular motion
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Tracked particle moves only up and down (vertical arrow). Wave moves rightward (horizontal arrow). The two motions are perpendicular.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The particle moves ONLY perpendicular to the wave direction. It does NOT travel horizontally. This is the defining property of a transverse wave: particle displacement ⊥ wave propagation.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — adjust tension
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to change string tension T. Higher tension = faster wave (v = √(T/μ)). Particle still moves only vertically.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Increasing tension increases wave speed but does NOT change the perpendicular nature of particle motion. v = √(T/μ) — tension and linear mass density determine speed.

trigger_keywords: particle wave ke saath jaata hai
particle moves horizontally in transverse wave
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Particles in a transverse wave travel along the wave direction (horizontally)
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: In a transverse wave, each particle executes SHM perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. The particle oscillates about its mean position and does not travel with the wave. Only the wave energy propagates, not the particles.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Track one particle showing it moves only up and down. Draw perpendicular arrows: orange vertical (particle motion) and blue horizontal (wave propagation).
